222 
Optimizing Technical Documentation for RAG Systems: A Comparative 
Analysis of Semantic Chunking vs. Structural Formatting 
Aashish Dhakal1, Shashank Shree Neupane1, Aadarsha Dhakal2, Muktinath Paudel1 
1Department of Information Technology, Presidential Graduate School, Kathmandu, Nepal 
2STARTsmall Pvt Ltd 
 
A R T I C L E I N F O  A B S T R A C T 
Article History: 
Accepted : 01 Jan 2026 
Published: 25 Jan 2026 
 
 As software documentation increasingly serves as the knowledge base for 
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, technical writers face a 
dilemma: should content be optimiz ed for human readability or machine 
re- trievability?

metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010
metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com
metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET

retrieved_chunks.text:
The ―Lost in the Middle‖ Phenomenon 
Liu et al. [4] demonstrated that LLMs stru ggle to 
access information located in the middle of long input 
contexts. This finding supports the argu - ment for 
―Chunking‖, breaking long documents into smaller  
segments. However, the  optimal granularity of 
chunking remains a subject of debate. Too small, and 
the chunk lacks context; too large, and the signal is 
diluted.

International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology | www.ijsrset.com | Vol 13 | Issue 1 
Aashish Dhakal et al Int J Sci Res Sci Eng Technol, January-February-2026, 13 (1) : 222-227 
 
 
 
 
 227 
The era of writing  for humans alone is over; we must 
now write for the vector space they inhabit. REFERENCES 
 
[1]. P. Aggarwal, V. M. Vishwamitra, and R. R. Shah, ―Geo: Generative engine optimization,‖ 
arXiv preprint arXiv:2311.09735, 2023. [2].

retrieved_chunks.text:
International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology | www.ijsrset.com | Vol 13 | Issue 1 
Aashish Dhakal et al Int J Sci Res Sci Eng Technol, January-February-2026, 13 (1) : 222-227 
 
 
 
 
 225 
Author 
(Year) 
Core Focus Key Contribution Limitation Addressed by This Study 
Aggarwal et 
al. (2023) [1] 
Content 
Optimization 
Coined ―GEO‖ (Generative 
Engine Optimization) for AI 
visibility. Focuses on the Generative phase 
(tone/authority), whereas we focus on the 
*Retrieval* phase (vector math). A. Win Rate Analysis 
The ―Win Rate‖ represents the percentage of queries 
where a specific structure type appeared at Rank #1. TABLE 2 WIN RATE BY STRUCTURE TYPE (TOP-1 
ACCURACY) 
Structure Type Win Rate (%) 
Standard Prose (Chunked) 28.0% 
Standard Prose (Whole) 24.0% 
Q&A Format (Whole) 24.0% 
Fact-Dense (Whole) 20.0% 
Structured JSON (Whole) 4.0% 
 
As shown in Table 2, Semantically Chunked Prose  
achieved the highest Win Rate. This confirms the 
hypothesis that high -granularity indexing allows the  
retriever to ―snipe‖ specific queries. When a doc- 
ument is split into sentences, the vector for a specific 
sentence (e.g., ―Provisioned concurrency fixes cold 
starts‖) is mathematically much closer to the query 
vector than a vector representing a whole paragraph 
containing five different facts. B. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) Analysis 
MRR meas ures the overall ranking health. A struc - 
ture that consistently appears at Rank 2 or 3 is often 
more valuable in production RAG systems than one 
that is either Rank 1 or Rank 100. TABLE 3 MEAN RECIPROCAL RANK (MRR) 
Structure Type MRR Score 
Q&A Format (Whole) 0.541 
Standard Prose (Whole) 0.527 
Fact-Dense (Whole) 0.427 
Standard Prose (Chunked) 0.398 
Structured JSON (Whole) 0.260 
The data in Table 3 reveals a fascinating inversion. While Chunked Prose won on precision, it failed on 
consistency (MRR 0.398). Q&A Formatting emerged as 
the most stable structure (MRR 0.541).

metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com
metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET
metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010

baseline.answer: The main objective of this document appears to be comparing Semantic Chunking and Structural Formatting in optimizing technical documentation for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, with a focus on determining which method is more effective in terms of precision (Win Rate) and consistency (Mean Reciprocal Rank or MRR).

retrieved_chunks.text:
METHODOLOGY  
A. Theoretical Framework 
We model the retrieval task as a Nearest Neighbor 
Search (NNS) problem in a vector space Rd. Let D ={d1, 
d2, ..., dn} be the set of document segments. Let q be 
the user query. We define an embedding function E : 
text → R384. The relevance score S is defined as the 
Cosine Similarity: 
S(q, d ) =  E(q) · E(di)  
i
  E(q)  E(di)     (1) 
Our objective is to find the structure type T that 
maximizes S(q, di) across a distribution of queries.

metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET
metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010
metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com

improved.answer: The main objective of this document, as stated in the context, is to find the structure type T that maximizes the relevance score S(q, di) across a distribution of queries. This is achieved by using an embedding function E and defining the relevance score as the Cosine Similarity between the query (q) and each document segment (di).

retrieved_chunks.text:
B. The ―Hybrid GEO‖ Proposal 
Based on this da ta, we propose a hybrid 
documentation standard. Technical writers should 
not choose between Prose or Q&A; they should layer 
them. Recommendation: Write the main body of 
documentation in natural prose (to be chunked by the 
RAG pipeline) for precision. However, append a 
Frequently Asked Questions section at the bottom of 
the page. This Q&A section acts as a ―Retrieval An- 
chor,‖ ensuring that if t he chunking strategy fails to 
capture the nuance, the explicit Q&A pair will catch 
the query vector. C. Implications for API Documentation 
Current API documentation (Swagger/OpenAPI) re- 
lies heavily on structured tables and JSON schemas. Our research sugges ts these formats are largely in - 
visible to RAG systems. To make API docs ―AI - 
Ready,‖ engineers must generate natural language 
descriptions alongside the schema definitions. D.

metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET
metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010
metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com

retrieved_chunks.text:
1. AWS Cloud Architecture: (Topic: Cold Starts) 
2. Green Computing: (Topic: Power Usage Ef- 
fectiveness - PUE) 
3. Django Framework: (Topic: N+1 Query Prob- 
lem) 
4. Blockchain Technology: (Topic: Proof of 
Stake Consensus) 
5. AI Architecture: (Topic: RAG Components) 
C. Corpus Preparation and Chunking Strategy 
For each domain, we created five variations of the 
content. To ensure scientific validity, the semantic 
information (the core facts) remained identical across 
all variations; only the structure changed. For the ―Chunked‖ variations, we utilized a Recur- sive 
Character Text Splitter with the following hy- 
perparameters: 
• Chunk Size: 512 characters. • Chunk Overlap: 50 characters. • Separators: Paragraph breaks ‗\n\n‗ followed by 
sentence terminators. This configuration was chosen to approximate the 
standard retrieval window used in commercial RAG 
pipelines (e.g., LlamaIndex default settings). D.

metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010
metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET
metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com

retrieved_chunks.text:
Variable Definitions 
1) Standard Prose (Whole). A continuous narra - 
tive paragraph typical of blog posts. Indexed as a 
sin- gle vector. 2) Standard Prose (Chunked). The same narrative 
paragraph, but  split into atomic semantic units. This tests the hypothesis that smaller vectors are 
more pre- cise. 3) Q&A Format. Explicit Question-Answer pairs 
(e.g., ―Q: What causes latency? A: Latency is 
caused by...‖). This structure attempts to mirror 
the user‘s query embedding within the document 
itself. 4) Fact-Dense List. A bulleted list of key-value 
pairs (e.g., ―* Cause: Cold Start‖). This removes 
stop words to test if ―fluff‖ dilutes vector quality. 5) Structured JSON. A valid JSON -LD object (e.g., 
{"cause": "cold start" }). This tests if machine -
readable schema s commonly used for Google 
Rich Snippets are effective in vector search. RESULTS 
We analyzed the results using two primary metrics: 
Win Rate (Precision) and Mean Reciprocal Rank 
(Consistency). TABLE 1 COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS OF RELATED LITERATURE 
Author 
(Year) 
Core Focus Key Contribution Limitation Addressed by This Study 
Reimers et al. (2019) [3] 
Model 
Architecture 
Introduced SBERT, enabling 
efficient cosine similarity for 
sentence pairs. Focuses on model training, not on how 
to structure the input text for optimal 
embed- ding. Lewis et al. (2020) [2] 
System 
Architecture 
Defined th e  R A G  f r a m ework 
combining dense retrieval with 
generative models. Assumes standard Wikipedia-style prose; 
does not test technical documentation 
 formats (JSON/Code).

metadata.subject: Research Paper

metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET
metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010

retrieved_chunks.text:
International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology | www.ijsrset.com | Vol 13 | Issue 1 
Aashish Dhakal et al Int J Sci Res Sci Eng Technol, January-February-2026, 13 (1) : 222-227 
 
 
 
 
 223 
Tools like GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and Per- plexity 
allow developers to query documentation us- ing 
natural language (e.g., ―How do I configure AWS 
Lambda cold starts?‖ ) and receive synthesized an - 
swers generated by Large Language Models (LLMs) [1]. These systems rely on Retrieval-Augmented Gen- 
eration (RAG) architectures. In a RAG system, the 
―intelligence‖ of the answer is strictly bounded by the 
performance of the ―Retriever‖— the component 
responsible for finding the r elevant documentation 
snippet from a massive vector database [2]. If the cor- 
rect documentation is not retrieved in the top k results 
(typically k = 3 or k = 5), the LLM will hallucinate or 
fail to answer. This creates a new optimization challenge for 
technical writers and DevRel engineers, a field 
now emerging as ―Generative Engine Optimiza - tion‖ 
(GEO). Traditional Search Engine Optimiza - tion 
(SEO) techniques, such as keyword stuffing  and 
backlink building, are ill -suited for the high - 
dimensional ve ctor spaces that power RAG. An em - 
bedding model does not count keywords; it measures 
semantic density and intent alignment. Furthermore, the industry lacks empirical guidance on 
how to structure content for this new medium. Documentation is often stored in structured formats 
like JSON or YAML for API references, or Mark- 
down for guides. Does an embedding model prefer a 
bulleted list? A JSON object? A narrative paragraph? This paper addresses this gap by conducting a com- 
parative analysis of document structures. We posit 
that the physical structure of text its syntax, density, 
and formatting plays a decisive role in its vector 
representation and, consequently, its retrievability. RELATED WORK 
The optimization of text for machine retrieval is well- 
studied in the context of keyword search (BM25), but 
the shift to dense vector retrieval introduces new 
variables. A. Literature Review Summary 
Table I provides a comparative analysis of key litera- 
ture that frames this study. While previous work has 
focused on the architecture of the *model* (SBERT, 
DPR) or the *generation* style (GEO), there is a dis- 
tinct lack of research on the *input data structure* for 
documentation specifically. B. Neural Information Retrieval 
The field of Information Retrieval (IR) has moved 
from sparse representations (TF-IDF, BM25) to dense 
vector representations [5]. Reimers and Gurevych 
[3] introduced Sentence-BERT (SBERT), which uses 
siamese networks to derive semantically meaningful 
sentence embeddings. This architecture forms the 
backbone of most modern RAG systems and is the 
basis for the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model used in this 
study. C.

metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010
metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET
metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com

baseline.answer:
The key points discussed in the document are as follows:

1. The Hybrid GEO Proposal for a hybrid documentation standard that layers natural prose and Q&A formats.
2. The implications for API Documentation, suggesting the need to generate natural language descriptions alongside schema definitions to make them AI-Ready.
3. A research study on the structure of text in relation to its vector representation and retrievability, focusing on document structures like Standard Prose (Whole/Chunked), Q&A Format, Fact-Dense List, Structured JSON, and their impact on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems.
4. The study's methodology, which includes creating five variations of content for each domain, analyzing the results using Win Rate (Precision) and Mean Reciprocal Rank, and comparing it with related literature.
5. The lack of empirical guidance on how to structure content for RAG systems, and the need to address this gap by conducting a comparative analysis of document structures.
6. The shift from sparse representations to dense vector representations in Information Retrieval (IR), with Sentence-BERT (SBERT) being introduced as the backbone of most modern RAG systems.

improved.answer:
The key points discussed in the document by Liu et al. (2023) are as follows:

1. Long-term memory (LLMs) recall start and end information better than middle information, which is often referred to as the "Lost in the Middle" phenomenon.
2. This finding highlights the need for chunking or organizing information into smaller parts to improve memory retention.
3. However, the document does not prescribe a specific formatting standard for this chunking process.

222 
Optimizing Technical Documentation for RAG Systems: A Comparative 
Analysis of Semantic Chunking vs. Structural Formatting 
Aashish Dhakal1, Shashank Shree Neupane1, Aadarsha Dhakal2, Muktinath Paudel1 
1Department of Information Technology, Presidential Graduate School, Kathmandu, Nepal 
2STARTsmall Pvt Ltd 
 
A R T I C L E I N F O  A B S T R A C T 
Article History: 
Accepted : 01 Jan 2026 
Published: 25 Jan 2026 
 
 As software documentation increasingly serves as the knowledge base for 
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, technical writers face a 
dilemma: should content be optimiz ed for human readability or machine 
re- trievability?

metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com
metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET
metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010

baseline.answer:
The conclusions and recommendations provided are as follows:

1. Based on the win rate analysis, semantically chunked prose achieved the highest precision, confirming the hypothesis that high-granularity indexing allows the retriever to "snipe" specific queries.

2. However, in terms of consistency (Mean Reciprocal Rank or MRR), Q&A formatting emerged as the most stable structure.

3. Therefore, a hybrid documentation standard is proposed where technical writers should layer both prose and Q&A. The main body of documentation should be written in natural prose for precision, while an FAQ section should be appended at the bottom of the page to act as a "Retrieval Anchor" if the chunking strategy fails to capture nuance.

4. For API documentation, it is suggested that engineers generate natural language descriptions alongside schema definitions to make API docs more visible to RAG systems.

metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010
metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET
metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com

retrieved_chunks.text:
The Agentic Paradox: Retrieval vs. Action 
A nuance emerging from this study is the distinc- tion 
between RAG (Retrieval) and Agents (Action). While 
our data proves JSON is poor for retrieval, it remains 
the standard for function calling by Agents (e.g., 
OpenAI Functions). This creates a paradox: d ocumentation needs to be 
Prose to be found, but JSON to be used. We suggest a 
dual-layer approach for technical documentation 
sites: 1. Visible Layer: Natural language descriptions 
and Q&A for the Vector Index. 2. Hidden Layer: 
JSON schemas wrapped in HTML <details> tags or 
separate metadata fields, which are excluded from the 
embedding process but passed to the LLM context once 
the document is retrieved. CONCLUSION  AND FUTURE  WORK 
We have demonstrated that the structure of technical 
documentation significantly influences its retrievability 
in RAG systems. Contrary to traditional data modeling 
best practices, unstructured natural language 
(specifically Q&A pairs) outperforms structured data 
(JSON) in vector search tasks. Future work should investigate the role of Cross - 
Encoders (Re-ranking models). While this study 
focused on the bi -encoder retrieval step, it is possible 
that a powerful re -ranker could rescue the poor 
performance of JSON content if it reaches the 
candidate generation  stage. Additionally, the impact 
of Hybrid Search (combining BM25 keywords with 
Vectors) on these structural findings warrants further 
exploration. The shift to AI -mediated documentation 
consumption requires a shift in writing standards.

metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010
metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com
metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET

improved.answer:
The conclusions and recommendations provided are as follows:

1. A hybrid documentation standard is proposed, where technical writers should layer natural prose and a Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) section. The main body of the documentation should be written in natural prose, while an FAQ section should be appended at the bottom of the page to act as a Retrieval Anchor.

2. For API documentation, engineers are recommended to generate natural language descriptions alongside schema definitions to make API docs AI-Ready.

3. The paper suggests a dual-layer approach for technical documentation sites: a visible layer with natural language descriptions and Q&A for the Vector Index, and a hidden layer with JSON schemas wrapped in HTML <details> tags or separate metadata fields, which are excluded from the embedding process but passed to the LLM context once the document is retrieved.

4. Future work should investigate the role of Cross-Encoders (Re-ranking models) and the impact of Hybrid Search on these structural findings.

5. The shift to AI-mediated documentation consumption requires a shift in writing standards.

C. Corpus Preparation and Chunking Strategy 
For each domain, we created five variations of the 
content. To ensure scientific validity, the semantic 
information (the core facts) remained identical across 
all variations; only the structure changed. For the ―Chunked‖ variations, we utilized a Recur- sive 
Character Text Splitter with the following hy- 
perparameters: 
• Chunk Size: 512 characters. • Chunk Overlap: 50 characters. • Separators: Paragraph breaks ‗\n\n‗ followed by 
sentence terminators. This configuration was chosen to approximate the 
standard retrieval window used in commercial RAG 
pipelines (e.g., LlamaIndex default settings). D.

Optimizing Technical Documentation for RAG Systems: A Comparative 
Analysis of Semantic Chunking vs. Structural Formatting 
Aashish Dhakal1, Shashank Shree Neupane1, Aadarsha Dhakal2, Muktinath Paudel1 
1Department of Information Technology, Presidential Graduate School, Kathmandu, Nepal 
2STARTsmall Pvt Ltd 
 
A R T I C L E I N F O  A B S T R A C T 
Article History: 
Accepted : 01 Jan 2026 
Published: 25 Jan 2026 
 
 As software documentation increasingly serves as the knowledge base for 
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, technical writers face a 
dilemma: should content be optimiz ed for human readability or machine 
re- trievability?

retrieved_chunks.text:
The ―Lost in the Middle‖ Phenomenon 
Liu et al. [4] demonstrated that LLMs stru ggle to 
access information located in the middle of long input 
contexts. This finding supports the argu - ment for 
―Chunking‖, breaking long documents into smaller  
segments. However, the  optimal granularity of 
chunking remains a subject of debate. Too small, and 
the chunk lacks context; too large, and the signal is 
diluted.

retrieved_chunks.text:
This study investigates the impact of document structure 
(Standard Prose, Q&A Pairs, Fact -Dense Lists, and Structured JSON) and 
indexing strategies (Whole Document vs. Semantic Chunking) on vector 
retrieval performance. Using a controlled experiment with 250 retrieval 
trials across five technical domains (e.g., AWS Architecture, Django ORM) 
and the all -MiniLM-L6-v2 embedding model, we demonstrate a critical 
trade- off between precision and ranking stabil ity. Results indicate that 
Semantically Chunked Prose yields the highest precision (Top-1 Win Rate: 
28%), effectively ―sniping‖ specific queries. However, Question -Answer 
(Q&A) for - matting offers superior ranking consistency (MRR: 0.54), 
acting as a retri eval safety net. Conversely, structured data formats like 
JSON-LD, despite their utility in traditional schema parsing, incur a 
significant penalty in vec - tor retrieval (Win Rate: 4%), revealing a 
―Semantic Gap‖ in current embedding architectures. We conc lude that 
technical documentation pipelines must evolve to prioritize natural 
language Q&A structures over purely syntactic schemas to maximize 
retrievability in RAG architectures. Keywords: Research Paper, Technical Documentation, RAG, Information 
Retrieval, Semantic Chunking, Vector Search, Knowledge Management. Publication Issue : 
Volume 13, Issue 1 
January-February-2026 
 
Page Number : 
222-227 
 
INTRODUCTION 
The consumption of technical documentation is un - 
dergoing a fundamental paradigm shift. Histori- cally, 
developers navigated documentation via man- ual 
search: entering keywords into a search bar, scan- ning 
a list of blue links, and reading long -form arti- cles to 
extract relevant snippets. Today, this work - flow is 
being rapidly replaced by AI-mediated inter- actions.

baseline.answer: The chunking strategy affects retrieval quality positively, as demonstrated in the study. Semantically Chunked Prose yields the highest precision (Top-1 Win Rate: 28%) and effectively "snipes" specific queries. However, it's important to note that Question-Answer formatting offers superior ranking consistency (MRR: 0.54), acting as a retrieval safety net. The optimal granularity of chunking is crucial; too small may lack context, while too large may dilute the signal.

retrieved_chunks.text:
Variable Definitions 
1) Standard Prose (Whole). A continuous narra - 
tive paragraph typical of blog posts. Indexed as a 
sin- gle vector. 2) Standard Prose (Chunked). The same narrative 
paragraph, but  split into atomic semantic units. This tests the hypothesis that smaller vectors are 
more pre- cise. 3) Q&A Format. Explicit Question-Answer pairs 
(e.g., ―Q: What causes latency? A: Latency is 
caused by...‖). This structure attempts to mirror 
the user‘s query embedding within the document 
itself. 4) Fact-Dense List. A bulleted list of key-value 
pairs (e.g., ―* Cause: Cold Start‖). This removes 
stop words to test if ―fluff‖ dilutes vector quality. 5) Structured JSON. A valid JSON -LD object (e.g., 
{"cause": "cold start" }). This tests if machine -
readable schema s commonly used for Google 
Rich Snippets are effective in vector search. RESULTS 
We analyzed the results using two primary metrics: 
Win Rate (Precision) and Mean Reciprocal Rank 
(Consistency). TABLE 1 COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS OF RELATED LITERATURE 
Author 
(Year) 
Core Focus Key Contribution Limitation Addressed by This Study 
Reimers et al. (2019) [3] 
Model 
Architecture 
Introduced SBERT, enabling 
efficient cosine similarity for 
sentence pairs. Focuses on model training, not on how 
to structure the input text for optimal 
embed- ding. Lewis et al. (2020) [2] 
System 
Architecture 
Defined th e  R A G  f r a m ework 
combining dense retrieval with 
generative models. Assumes standard Wikipedia-style prose; 
does not test technical documentation 
 formats (JSON/Code).

retrieved_chunks.text:
International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology | www.ijsrset.com | Vol 13 | Issue 1 
Aashish Dhakal et al Int J Sci Res Sci Eng Technol, January-February-2026, 13 (1) : 222-227 
 
 
 
 
 226 
As visualized in Fig. 1, the JSON object resides in a 
distant cluster in the vector space, likely group - ing 
with other code -heavy segments rather than  the 
natural language query. D. Latency and Token Efficiency 
Beyond accuracy, we  observed implications for sys- 
tem efficiency. JSON  and XML formats incur a ―To- 
ken Tax.‖  The structural overhead (brackets, clos ing 
tags) increased the token count by an ave rage of 34% 
compared to the Fact -Dense List format. In a large -
scale RAG system billing by token usage (e.g., OpenAI 
embeddings API), storing documentation in JSON 
format not only degrades retrieval accuracy but also 
increases operational costs by over 30%. DISCUSSION  
A. The Precision-Consistency Trade-off 
Our findings highlight a fundamental trade -off in 
RAG optimization: 
The Sniper Strategy (Chunking): Optimiz- ing for 
Chunked Prose maximizes the chance of being the 
primary citation (Rank 1). This is ideal  for specific, 
fact-based queries (e.g., error codes). • The Safety Net Strategy (Q&A): Optimizing for 
Q&A format maximizes the chance of being 
included in the context window (Top 3), even if 
not at Rank 1. This is ideal for broad, conceptual 
queries.

improved.answer: The chunking strategy affects retrieval quality by optimizing for precision, particularly for specific, fact-based queries. By splitting a narrative paragraph into atomic semantic units (Chunked Standard Prose), it increases the chance of being the primary citation (Rank 1) in a retrieval system, making it more suitable for queries seeking precise information such as error codes. However, this strategy may not be ideal for broader or conceptual queries due to its focus on smaller vectors and precision.
